Name: curvature_aware_precision_switching
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Switching: Training-Free Adaptive Inference via Hessian-Guided Precision Routing
Short Hypothesis: The local curvature of the loss landscape, quantified by Hessian eigenvalues, serves as a direct proxy for precision sensitivity, enabling training-free adaptive precision switching that optimally balances computational efficiency and model performance.
Related Work: Recent work has explored training-free precision allocation based on activation geometry, such as Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) [1], which uses activation covariance condition numbers to guide precision switching. However, these methods focus on activation statistics rather than the underlying loss landscape curvature. Our proposal distinguishes itself by leveraging Hessian-guided precision switching, which directly ties precision allocation to the optimization landscape's geometry, providing a more theoretically grounded and empirically robust approach.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference offers significant computational savings but typically relies on static profiling or costly calibration. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Switching (CAPS), a training-free framework that dynamically adjusts numerical precision based on the local curvature of the loss landscape, quantified by Hessian eigenvalues. We hypothesize that regions with high curvature (large Hessian eigenvalues) are more sensitive to precision reduction, while low-curvature regions can tolerate lower precision without compromising performance. CAPS computes Hessian-vector products on-the-fly using randomized numerical linear algebra, mapping curvature estimates to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground CAPS with PAC-Bayes bounds linking curvature to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPS achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, CAPS provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Curvature-Precision Sensitivity Analysis: Measure the impact of precision reduction on model performance across regions with varying curvature. Use randomized Hessian-vector products to estimate curvature and evaluate accuracy degradation under different precision levels.

Experiment 2: Dynamic Precision Switching: Implement CAPS on a single-GPU setup and compare its throughput-accuracy trade-offs against static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. Use WILDS benchmarks to evaluate performance under distribution shifts.

Experiment 3: PAC-Bayes Generalization Bounds: Derive and empirically validate PAC-Bayes bounds linking curvature to generalization stability under quantization noise. Compare theoretical bounds with empirical results to assess the robustness of CAPS.
Experiment 4: Latency Budget Enforcement: Measure the computational overhead of CAPS and its ability to enforce strict latency budgets. Compare wall-clock latency and memory usage against baselines.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Computational Overhead: Randomized Hessian-vector products may introduce additional computational overhead, potentially offsetting the benefits of precision switching.
Curvature Estimation Accuracy: The accuracy of curvature estimates depends on the quality of randomized numerical linear algebra methods, which may vary across different architectures and tasks.
Generalization to New Tasks: While CAPS is designed to be task-agnostic, its effectiveness may vary across different domains and tasks, particularly those with highly non-linear loss landscapes.
Hardware Compatibility: The feasibility of CAPS depends on hardware support for mixed-precision computation, which may not be uniformly available across all GPU architectures.
